title: video Investment strategist debunks market 'narratives,' shares strategy for upcoming year Charles Schwab managing direc
raw_response: {"rating": 2.3, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "Content type: News report/compilation. The author’s headlines and blurbs consistently frame labor actions as disruptive to consumers and industry, and occasionally use business-friendly evaluative language, resulting in a Lean Right tilt. Given multiple recurring right-leaning framing choices and limited balancing context, the article merits a Lean Right rating of +2.3. Detailed Analysis: (1) Strike coverage emphasizes public harm with charged terms: “causing travel chaos for 130,000 passengers,” “forcing cancellation of 240 flights and stranding over 100,000 passengers,” and “impacting 350K customers,” prioritizing consumer disruption over workers’ grievances; (2) Labor actions are linked to national defense/priority programs: “next-gen fighter jet threatened by potential delays as Boeing workers go on strike,” “halts fighter jet assembly,” and “handling Trump fighter jet program braces for strike,” a framing likely to alienate readers from the strikers; (3) Unions are cast as unreasonable by spotlighting rejected offers: “rejection of contract offering 20% general wage increase and $5,000 bonus,” implying excess without providing workers’ counterarguments; (4) Business-friendly evaluative language appears: “Cannabis business owners have been saved from having to comply with Oregon’s Measure 119,” signaling approval of rolling back pro-union requirements; (5) Republican/Trump workforce efforts are framed positively: “administration prioritizes bolstering skilled trades workforce,” using favorable verbs without scrutiny; (6) An editorial stance favors market-oriented policy: “American manufacturing needs labor reform, not tariffs,” aligning with free-market/anti-tariff preferences; (7) Limited balancing context: workers’ rationales are mostly reduced to brief mentions like “stalled contract talks,” with few details on labor conditions or pro-union arguments, cumulatively tilting coverage to the right."}

parsed_output.explanation: Content type: News report/compilation. The author’s headlines and blurbs consistently frame labor actions as disruptive to consumers and industry, and occasionally use business-friendly evaluative language, resulting in a Lean Right tilt. Given multiple recurring right-leaning framing choices and limited balancing context, the article merits a Lean Right rating of +2.3. Detailed Analysis: (1) Strike coverage emphasizes public harm with charged terms: “causing travel chaos for 130,000 passengers,” “forcing cancellation of 240 flights and stranding over 100,000 passengers,” and “impacting 350K customers,” prioritizing consumer disruption over workers’ grievances; (2) Labor actions are linked to national defense/priority programs: “next-gen fighter jet threatened by potential delays as Boeing workers go on strike,” “halts fighter jet assembly,” and “handling Trump fighter jet program braces for strike,” a framing likely to alienate readers from the strikers; (3) Unions are cast as unreasonable by spotlighting rejected offers: “rejection of contract offering 20% general wage increase and $5,000 bonus,” implying excess without providing workers’ counterarguments; (4) Business-friendly evaluative language appears: “Cannabis business owners have been saved from having to comply with Oregon’s Measure 119,” signaling approval of rolling back pro-union requirements; (5) Republican/Trump workforce efforts are framed positively: “administration prioritizes bolstering skilled trades workforce,” using favorable verbs without scrutiny; (6) An editorial stance favors market-oriented policy: “American manufacturing needs labor reform, not tariffs,” aligning with free-market/anti-tariff preferences; (7) Limited balancing context: workers’ rationales are mostly reduced to brief mentions like “stalled contract talks,” with few details on labor conditions or pro-union arguments, cumulatively tilting coverage to the right.
